title: Mum's 'tears of joy' as her daughter finally leaves hospital unit

input.lead: Natasha spent more than 14 months in hospital due to uncertainty about who should fund her complex care
input.summary: Natasha spent more than 14 months in hospital due to uncertainty about who should fund her complex care
input.evidence_text: A woman who was stuck in hospital for months while the NHS worked out who should fund her complex care package has been allowed to leave, just days after her plight was highlighted by the BBC Natasha has a rare progressive neurological disease and requires one-to-one care jointly funded by her council and the local NHS Her mum, Elaine, had long battled to secure a care package for the 27-year-old from Southport and while one had been ready since May, Natasha was unable to move until a funding deal was reached "She's going to have an amazing life," said Elaine of Natasha's move. "I'm just so excited for her. I never believed this day was going to come." Natasha was born with a rare degenerative disorder called Leigh's Disease , external , which affects the brain and spinal cord She also has GAMT deficiency, which affects brain development and causes epileptic seizures

title: Warrington Council's sale of £30m Redwood Bank shares collapses

input.lead: The council says a further offer has been received, but there will be "new and significant costs"
input.summary: The council says a further offer has been received, but there will be "new and significant costs"
input.evidence_text: The planned sale of a council's £30m stake in a bank has fallen though, the authority confirmed Warrington Council bought a third share of Redwood Bank in 2017 and said earlier this year it had agreed to sell its share at a "substantial financial loss" . It has now said this sale has collapsed, because of "macroeconomic conditions" Warrington Council built up £1.8bn of debt linked to its investment strategy in recent years, which led to the government appointing ministerial envoys to work with the authority last year The authority said it has received a further offer for its shares in the bank, from a third party not linked to the original bid In a report to the council's cabinet ahead of a meeting on Monday (27 July), it said the offer would be "confirmed through due diligence" but no more details have been provided The report said the council had already incurred "significant costs" on matters relating to the sale, and that there would be "new and significant costs" linked to a new offer

final_reason: Dedupe: live item has no exact/history repeat; accepted for editorial selection. | Validator: story is centred outside Greater Manchester; publisher section chrome was ignored.

title: Burnham pledges to end rough sleeping in first speech as prime minister

input.lead: The Labour leader made a similar pledge as Greater Manchester mayor, although he failed to achieve this
input.summary: The Labour leader made a similar pledge as Greater Manchester mayor, although he failed to achieve this
input.evidence_text: Andy Burnham has made ending rough sleeping in the country his first policy pledge as the new prime minister Making his first speech in Downing Street, Burnham said he wanted the government to be "investing in people's success rather than paying for failure" The government said an additional £340m will be provided to support the policy, which is expected to provide 1,200 homes and "intensive support" for at least 3,000 people in England over a five-year period When he became Greater Manchester mayor in 2017, Burnham pledged to end rough sleeping in the area by 2020 , external . Figures published in 2025 showed rough sleeping had increased year-on-year, although decreased compared to 2017 The government said the first steps will involve helping those sleeping rough for a long period of time to move into secure housing, and includes the "acquisition of suitable homes" Burnham, in a statement after his first speech outside 10 Downing Street, said: "On my first day as prime minister, I'm announcing this government's commitment to finally end long-term rough sleeping across the UK at the earliest opportunity

title: Key takeaways as Andy Burnham becomes PM

input.lead: The new Labour leader has formally taken office and set out his priorities for power in No 10
input.summary: The new Labour leader has formally taken office and set out his priorities for power in No 10
input.evidence_text: The former mayor of Greater Manchester becomes the country's fourth prime minister in five years and the seventh since 2016 Live: Andy Burnham sets out his priorities as prime minister Burnham says rough sleeping and the cost of living are priorities In his first speech in Downing Street, Burnham announced he would "end rough sleeping in our country" This was swiftly followed by a No 10 press release promising an extra £340m for the task over the next five years from within the housing department budget He also promised a new 10-year plan for Britain - to be unveiled later this year - and to build a new economy, putting "life's essentials back under public control" But arguing that people also needed "breathing space now", he said announcements on tackling the cost of living would start on Tuesday

title: Two arrested after police find guns buried in Eccles garden

input.lead: A man and a woman are taken into custody after the search of a home in Eccles, Greater Manchester
input.summary: A man and a woman are taken into custody after the search of a home in Eccles, Greater Manchester
input.evidence_text: Two people have been arrested after a 12-day police search of a home where guns were discovered buried in the back garden A shotgun, handgun and ammunition were dug up by Greater Manchester Police from under a lawn at the house on Crowngreen Road, Eccles Police, who had been at the home since 8 July, used heavy machinery to search the garden, and also discovered a machete and a Class B drug from inside the home The force said a 57-year-old man and a 54-year-old woman were arrested earlier on suspicion of possession of a firearm and ammunition and taken into custody for questioning The force said the firearms were though to be viable, and have been "made safe and removed from the address for forensic testing" Det Sgt Gareth Bannister said: "Firearms have absolutely no place on our streets and the recovery of these items is significant in removing potentially lethal weapons from our communities

title: Why voting method change matters in mayoral poll

input.lead: Why the Supplementary Vote may allow voters to vote with their heads and their hearts
input.summary: Why the Supplementary Vote may allow voters to vote with their heads and their hearts
input.evidence_text: The Labour Party could benefit most from the return of the Supplementary Votes (SV) system in the Greater Manchester mayoral race, a polling expert has said Voters in the region head to the polls on 30 July and will be able to select a first and second choice of candidate as SV comes into replace the first-past-the-post (FPTP) system used at the last election A number of candidates have already declared for the contest , triggered after Andy Burnham's successful campaign to become the MP of Makerfield, leaving the post he has held since 2017 vacant Prof Jonathan Tonge of the University of Liverpool said Labour could pick up second preference votes from Greens and Lib Dems, while Reform could be less likely to see Restore voters put them as second preference due to "bad blood" between the party leaders The system was used in the first two mayoral elections, but was scrapped by the last Conservative government in favour of FPTP system, before it was reintroduced again by Labour Under SCV, candidates need to win more than 50% of the vote in the first round to be come mayor or the top two candidates go into a run-off where second preference votes from eliminated candidates are counted

final_reason: Dedupe: live item has no exact/history repeat; accepted for editorial selection. | Validator: news item is 17 days old (hard cutoff 14d), dropped regardless of update wording.

title: What is the Greater Manchester mayor and what do they do?

input.lead: Greater Manchester goes to the polls on 30 July, but what powers does the region's mayor hold?
input.summary: Greater Manchester goes to the polls on 30 July, but what powers does the region's mayor hold?
input.evidence_text: Andy Burnham's victory in the Makerfield by-election means he has to step down as Greater Manchester mayor Burnham has been mayor since the role was first created in 2017 and the race to become his successor will be fiercely contested by all involved But what is the role they will be fighting for? The role of mayor was created as part of a law passed in 2016, one of a number of big pieces of devolution legislation brought in by the Conservatives after taking power in 2010 In a 2012 referendum, Manchester rejected having a directly-elected city mayor, along with eight other major cities, but the decision to have a regional or metropolitan mayor was directly negotiated between the government and the 10 Greater Manchester councils Andy Burnham, who left Parliament to stand for the role, was chosen as Labour's candidate for the first mayoral election in 2017 and won, taking 63.4% of the vote

final_reason: Без новых фактов: уже был 2026-06-20 как «What is the Greater Manchester mayor and what do they do?».

title: Greater Manchester's mayoral election explained

input.lead: Voters in all 10 boroughs will elect a new mayor to replace Andy Burnham on 30 July
input.summary: Voters in all 10 boroughs will elect a new mayor to replace Andy Burnham on 30 July
input.evidence_text: People living in Greater Manchester will head to the polls in July to vote for who they think should be the region's mayor Residents living in all 10 boroughs will vote in the Greater Manchester mayoral by-election on 30 July We take a look at why it is happening, what the timetable is, and how the supplementary vote system works The election was triggered when Andy Burnham, who held the office for nine years, was elected the MP for Makerfield in last Thursday's parliamentary by-election By law, an MP cannot sit in the House of Commons and hold a metropolitan mayor role that has powers over local policing The Greater Manchester mayoral contest has to take place within 35 days of the post becoming vacant

title: Free bikes offered to commuters hit by Metrolink tram disruption

input.lead: Passengers affected by the suspension of trams in Greater Manchester are offered free bike use
input.summary: Passengers affected by the suspension of trams in Greater Manchester are offered free bike use
input.evidence_text: Commuters in Greater Manchester who have been forced on to replacement buses during a major track overhaul have been offered up to 20 minutes of free travel on rental bikes The Altrincham, Eccles, and Trafford Centre Metrolink lines have been shut since 13 July for a £150m project to replace outdated track lines between Deansgate and Trafford Bar Lines to East Didsbury and Manchester Airport have also been affected, with passengers to the city centre facing major disruption until the scheme ends on 2 August Danny Vaughan, of Transport for Greater Manchester, said those affected could now make use of Starling Bikes every day for up to 20 minutes, and added "why not give active travel a go?" He said "there's never a good time to close parts of the network" and admitted the track project replacement had caused "a big impact" The pedal bikes are available to hire across the city, but have now been made available in Firswood, Chorlton and Salford Quays for 10 minutes free each way for an inbound and outbound journey, he said

title: Leaky dams on Holcombe Moor to stop flooding and wildfires

input.lead: The dams are designed to hold back the flow of rainwater on peat land preventing fires and floods
input.summary: The dams are designed to hold back the flow of rainwater on peat land preventing fires and floods
input.evidence_text: Leaky dams on Holcombe Moor to stop flooding and wildfires - BBC News Image caption, The leaky dams were created by hammering wooden posts into a gulley on the moors above Ramsbottom. By Richard Stead North West Published 20 July 2026 A series of 'leaky dams' have been created on the moors surrounding Greater Manchester to stop wildfires from spreading and to prevent flooding in the valleys below. The wooden dams have been installed in gullies by The National Trust and United Utilities across Holcombe Moor, part of the Stubbins Estate above Ramsbottom. Nik Taylor, 57, a National Trust Ranger for Holcombe Moor and the Stubbins Estate, said: "Keeping the moors wet by raising ground water levels is really important when we're in a period of hot weather because it can help create fire-breaks." United Utilities said using the peat land as "a natural sponge" can keep water on the moors giving multiple benefits. Image caption, Nick Taylor is a National Trust Ranger for Holcombe Moor and the Stubbins estate. Taylor said: "We use leaky dams to control the flow of water into the River Irwell and it's an essential part of the restoration of our peat lands. More than 1,000 acres of land at Holcombe Moor was transferred from the Ministry of Defence to the National Trust in 1994. The dams are designed to hold back the flow of rainwater which lands on the moors and subsequently enters the River Irwell catchment area. The work also involved planting sphagnum moss - a spongy wetland plant which is capable of holding up to 20 times its own weight in water. The sphagnum helps to create peat bogs, where dead vegetation accumulates rather than decays, capturing vast quantities of carbon in the ground. Image caption, Freya Bowden is a Master's student studying moorland management at the Univ

title: Email error sees students told they had failed to graduate

input.lead: Manchester Metropolitan University apologises after some students were mistakenly told they had failed
input.summary: Manchester Metropolitan University apologises after some students were mistakenly told they had failed

title: How town hall's ornate windows were turned into jewellery

input.lead: Jeweler Lin Fletcher gives a new lease of life to the 150-year-old stained glass from Manchester Town Hall
input.summary: Jeweler Lin Fletcher gives a new lease of life to the 150-year-old stained glass from Manchester Town Hall
input.evidence_text: How town hall's ornate windows were turned into jewellery - BBC News Image source, Manchester City Council Image caption, The original stained glass at Manchester Town Hall dated back to 1870s By Georgie Docker North West Published 20 July 2026 Fragments of coloured glass salvaged from the windows of a town hall undergoing a £500m restoration have been transformed into tiny pieces of jewellery. The collection of earrings, pendants and cuff-links made from the stained sheets of glass used in Manchester Town Hall was created by jeweller Lin Fletcher. The Grade-I I listed Victorian building has been shut since 2018 as work continues on a restoration project estimated to cost in the region of £520m. "I realised this waste glass from the Town Hall could be beautifully repurposed, giving Mancunians everywhere a chance to have a souvenir of the much-loved icon," Fletcher said. Image source, Geograph/David Dixon Image caption, The original stained-glass windows at Manchester Town Hall dated back to the Victorian era Fletcher set to work on a revamp of her own, cleaning and re-setting the stained glass from the windows, ready for a new purpose. The stained glass came from the more than 4,000 windows in the town hall, which was built in 1878 and is the ceremonial headquarters of Manchester City Council. Image source, Manchester City Council Image caption, The collection includes earrings, pendants, and cufflinks As part of the restoration works, the condition of the glass used in the original windows was assessed, with many pieces found to be in good condition. Fletcher crafted the jewellery using fragments of the original glass, alongside off-cuts from the authentic replacement glass which was specially created to replace the worn sections of the original windows A Manchester Ci

title: Woman charged with murdering pensioner found in Bolton flats

input.summary: A series of 'leaky dams' have been created on the moors surrounding Greater Manchester to stop wildfires from spreading and to prevent flooding in the valleys below The wooden dams have been installed in gullies by The National Trust and United Utilities across Holcombe Moor, par
input.evidence_text: A series of 'leaky dams' have been created on the moors surrounding Greater Manchester to stop wildfires from spreading and to prevent flooding in the valleys below The wooden dams have been installed in gullies by The National Trust and United Utilities across Holcombe Moor, part of the Stubbins Estate above Ramsbottom Nik Taylor, 57, a National Trust Ranger for Holcombe Moor and the Stubbins Estate, said: "Keeping the moors wet by raising ground water levels is really important when we're in a period of hot weather because it can help create fire-breaks." United Utilities said using the peat land as "a natural sponge" can keep water on the moors giving multiple benefits Taylor said: "We use leaky dams to control the flow of water into the River Irwell and it's an essential part of the restoration of our peat lands More than 1,000 acres of land at Holcombe Moor was transferred from the Ministry of Defence to the National Trust in 1994

input.evidence_text: Fragments of coloured glass salvaged from the windows of a town hall undergoing a £500m restoration have been transformed into tiny pieces of jewellery The collection of earrings, pendants and cuff-links made from the stained sheets of glass used in Manchester Town Hall was created by jeweller Lin Fletcher The Grade-I I listed Victorian building has been shut since 2018 as work continues on a restoration project estimated to cost in the region of £520m "I realised this waste glass from the Town Hall could be beautifully repurposed, giving Mancunians everywhere a chance to have a souvenir of the much-loved icon," Fletcher said Fletcher set to work on a revamp of her own, cleaning and re-setting the stained glass from the windows, ready for a new purpose The stained glass came from the more than 4,000 windows in the town hall, which was built in 1878 and is the ceremonial headquarters of Manchester City Council

A spokesperson for Bury Council said: "There are currently no plans to pursue a compulsory purchase order [on the building] due to the complex ownership arrangements." input.evidence_text: An inner-city part of Manchester that was a bustling hub for counterfeit goods before a major police crackdown has now emerged as "Britain's vape capital" Two narrow streets in Cheetham Hill are filled with bright, colourful shopfronts with names such as Vaporesso, Xhale and The Vape Boyz There are 51 shops selling vapes on Harris Street and Overbridge Road, something residents have said is "crazy", while a government minister has admitted it is "bizarre" The stores, which sell wholesale to customers coming from as far away as Glasgow and Edinburgh, emerged after 200 shops in the area were shut down in a two-year police operation to tackle the fake clothes trade and drug dealing The area had been described as the UK's counterfeit capital, with hundreds of millions of pounds worth of bogus goods seized by police as part of Operation Vulcan The crackdown saw Greater Manchester Police (GMP) make nearly 240 arrests and seize 1,050 tonnes of counterfeit items

Sadly, he was found dead at around 5pm on Sunday (July 19) A Greater Manchester Police spokesperson said his family has been informed. They are being supported by specialist officers Dozens of tributes for Michael, who was known as Mikey, have since been shared on social media since the news of his death. He had more than 21,000 followers on one of his TikTok pages and around 38,000 followers on another The last post he shared was six days ago. Michael was a boxer, the Manchester Evening News understands, and often posted about his fights on social media

input.evidence_text: Courts Dad of woman allegedly killed by husband tells jury: 'He is a two-faced person' Syed Ahmad denies murder News Amy Walker Court reporter 06:23, 21 Jul 2026 View 2 Images Police at the scene on Lulworth Gardens in Wythenshawe (Image: Jason Robert The dad of a woman allegedly murdered by her husband has told a trial: "He is a two-faced person." Syed Ahmad, 45, is charged with the murder of his wife Anam Rafay on January 27 this year. He denies the charge and is on trial at Manchester Crown Court Prosecutors allege that Mr Ahmad had been 'controlling and abusive' towards Mrs Rafay before she died. The couple were married by way of arranged marriage in 2005 when she was 16 years old In September last year she contacted a mufti, also known as a divorce mediator, in Longsight to ask about obtaining a divorce application, after becoming involved in a relationship with another man, jurors heard The following October she served that application upon Mr Ahmad who replied asking for mediation. She agreed to give him 'one more chance to change', but he never did, prosecutors allege

title: Dad initially told he had chest infection before hearing he had six months to live

input.lead: Glossop Dad initially told he had chest infection before hearing he had six months to live Glyn's terminal cancer diagnosis came as a 'complete shock' - now his son is taking on an inspirational challenge in his memory Comments News Hope Gallagher 06: A fit and healthy dad was di
input.summary: Glossop Dad initially told he had chest infection before hearing he had six months to live Glyn's terminal cancer diagnosis came as a 'complete shock' - now his son is taking on an inspirational challenge in his memory Comments News Hope Gallagher 06: A fit and healthy dad was di

His son Darell Smith, from Glossop, said that the diagnosis came as a 'complete shock' as his dad had originally been told he was just suffering from a chest infection Before this, Glyn was the person who would always be out walking and looking after the dog with an active lifestyle. However, when the cough did not seem to subside, Darell became increasingly worried and urged his father to go to the doctors It was then Glyn was sent for an X-ray where doctors identified a shadow on the lung and so was sent for further tests. The tests later confirmed the tragic news that Glyn had lung cancer and was told he had just six months to a year left to live and was placed into palliative ca Darell, 44, said: "It was a really tough time knowing that there would be no light at the end of the tunnel. We tried to stay positive as best we could, but we had times where we just couldn't digest what was happening."

In a new survey, conducted by watchdog Transport Focus, only 63% of respondents reported being satisfied wi In the survey, the motorway received its lowest scores in relation to journey times, with just 60% of respondents saying they were happy with the time it took to complete their journey using the M60 The orbital motorway however ranked slightly higher in relation to road surfaces and feeling safe, with 71% of people reporting they were satisfied with the M60 in these areas The report however comes as major work on the notorious Simister Island junction started earlier this month. While expenses are a key way of reimbursing employees for costs r In 2019, Oldham Council staff made 392 claims which cost over £132,000. While this dipped during the first year of the Covid-19 pandemic in 2020, this has largely risen since with the number of claims going up by 50 per cent In the financial year from April 2025 to March 2026, there were 612 claims made by staff costing the taxpayer more than £312,000. input.evidence_text: “I will put the care of people at the heart of everything I do. I will give this my all and I ask you all to pull with me,” he said It’s quite the pledge from the former mayor who promised to end rough sleeping in Greater Manchester by 2020 - and has, as yet, failed to achieve it. But that’s not to say he hasn’t made huge progress In the nine years since Mr Burnham took office he has set up ‘A Bed Every Night’ to get people off the streets by creating emergency accommodation and support and has consistently donated 15 per cent of his mayoral salary to the project. This initially led to a huge drop in the n But rough sleeping has not ended here and more recent figures show the issue is getting worse , with 197 people recorded as sleeping rough in 2025. And as I reported back in February, Manchester is at the sharp end of a refugee rough sleeping crisis caused by Britain’s broken asy The Greater Manchester picture however, is far better than the national view. The number of people rough sleeping in England fell from 4,751 in 2017 to a low of 2,443 in 2021. But it’s currently at 4,793 This morning, 78 CEOs and senior leaders of homelessness organisations from across England wrote to Mr Burnham calling for the government to adopt a national Housing First philosophy. That approach advocates for unconditional housing and wrap-around support and has been adopted i

Inspectors said there was an ‘unexplained rise’ amid a lack of oversight but the council said ‘there is no suggestion of misuse’ A report published ahead of an Oldham Council audit committee meeting on July 22 has revealed the outcome of a review into staff expenses by the local authority’s internal audit team which scrutinises council work. While expenses are a key way of reimbursing employees for costs r In 2019, Oldham Council staff made 392 claims which cost over £132,000. While this dipped during the first year of the Covid-19 pandemic in 2020, this has largely risen since with the number of claims going up by 50 per cent In the financial year from April 2025 to March 2026, there were 612 claims made by staff costing the taxpayer more than £312,000. Compared to 2019, this is a 26pc increase when adjusted for inflation The report from auditors said the reasons for this increase were ‘unclear’, adding: “There is a lack of regular monitoring of staff with high mileage claims or unusual travel patterns, as mileage spending is not routinely reported and trends over time are not reviewed

input.evidence_text: Manchester Metropolitan University Manchester Met University students wrongly told they'd failed their degree and could not attend graduation Students say they were left panicked and sobbing News Hope Gallagher 19:14, 20 Jul 2026 View 3 Images Manches Students from Manchester Metropolitan University were left in a state of panic after they received an email telling them that they had failed their degree just days before their graduation Students from the School of Education as well as from the Manchester Fashion Institute, which includes pathways like BA (Hons) Fashion, Fashion Design & Technology, Fashion Communication and International Fashion Business received an email telling them that they had failed to rec The email dropped into inboxes on Friday, just days before the undergraduates were set to have their big day. Many students took to social media to express their panic at the message, saying they were sobbing when they first received the email One student, Emily, whose graduation was four days after the email was sent, said: “Why do MMU think it’s okay to send an email to the entire cohort telling us we failed our degree and can’t go to graduation four days before? “Genuinely was balling my eyes out for a good 20 minutes thinking it was just me.”

input.evidence_text: Oldham Investigation finds 'unexplained' rise in Oldham Council staff expenses Oldham Council said there was 'no suggestion of misuse' and they 'are committed to maintaining the highest standards of accountability for public money' Comments News Edwar Issues have been raised about the thousands of pounds being claimed by Oldham Council staff in expenses. Inspectors said there was an ‘unexplained rise’ amid a lack of oversight but the council said ‘there is no suggestion of misuse’ A report published ahead of an Oldham Council audit committee meeting on July 22 has revealed the outcome of a review into staff expenses by the local authority’s internal audit team which scrutinises council work. While expenses are a key way of reimbursing employees for costs r In 2019, Oldham Council staff made 392 claims which cost over £132,000. While this dipped during the first year of the Covid-19 pandemic in 2020, this has largely risen since with the number of claims going up by 50 per cent In the financial year from April 2025 to March 2026, there were 612 claims made by staff costing the taxpayer more than £312,000. Compared to 2019, this is a 26pc increase when adjusted for inflation The report from auditors said the reasons for this increase were ‘unclear’, adding: “There is a lack of regular monitoring of staff with high mileage claims or unusual travel patterns, as mileage spending is not routinely reported and trends over time are not reviewed

input.summary: “I will put the care of people at the heart of everything I do. I will give this my all and I ask you all to pull with me,” he said It’s quite the pledge from the former mayor who promised to end rough sleeping in Greater Manchester by 2020 - and has, as yet, failed to achieve it
input.evidence_text: “I will put the care of people at the heart of everything I do. I will give this my all and I ask you all to pull with me,” he said It’s quite the pledge from the former mayor who promised to end rough sleeping in Greater Manchester by 2020 - and has, as yet, failed to achieve it. But that’s not to say he hasn’t made huge progress In the nine years since Mr Burnham took office he has set up ‘A Bed Every Night’ to get people off the streets by creating emergency accommodation and support and has consistently donated 15 per cent of his mayoral salary to the project. This initially led to a huge drop in the n But rough sleeping has not ended here and more recent figures show the issue is getting worse , with 197 people recorded as sleeping rough in 2025. And as I reported back in February, Manchester is at the sharp end of a refugee rough sleeping crisis caused by Britain’s broken asy The Greater Manchester picture however, is far better than the national view. The number of people rough sleeping in England fell from 4,751 in 2017 to a low of 2,443 in 2021. But it’s currently at 4,793 This morning, 78 CEOs and senior leaders of homelessness organisations from across England wrote to Mr Burnham calling for the government to adopt a national Housing First philosophy. That approach advocates for unconditional housing and wrap-around support and has been adopted i

title: The UK has had 58 Prime Ministers, but surprisingly few have been from the North

input.summary: Politics The UK has had 58 Prime Ministers, but surprisingly few have been from the North Before Andy Burnham you have to go back almost 200 years to find the last person from the area we now call Greater Manchester to sit in 10 Downing Street Comment Andy Burnham will become the
input.evidence_text: Politics The UK has had 58 Prime Ministers, but surprisingly few have been from the North Before Andy Burnham you have to go back almost 200 years to find the last person from the area we now call Greater Manchester to sit in 10 Downing Street Comment Andy Burnham will become the 58th Prime Minister of the UK when he is sworn into office on Monday afternoon But surprisingly few of those men and woman have been from the North of England. And even less have hailed from or represented the area we now call Greater Manchester In fact you have to go back almost 200 years to find the last person from round these parts to sit in 10 Downing Street. Bury -born Sir Robert Peel served as PM from 1834-35 and again from 1841-46 The father of the modern police force and one of the founders of the modern Conservative Party , Peel was the son of a wealthy textiles magnate and politician. It's thought he may have attended Bury Grammar School before attending Harrow and Oxford University A statue of Sir Robert now stands in Market Place in Bury town centre, the local Wetherspoons is named after him, while the Peel monument on Holcombe Hill near Ramsbottom , also bears his name

input.summary: Metrolink Bee Network offers people free 20-minute bicycle journey with Metrolink lines shut Transport for Greater Manchester have apologised for the disruption across Metrolink Comments News Edward Barnes Local Democracy Reporter 13:30, 20 Jul 2026 U People are being offered fre
input.evidence_text: Metrolink Bee Network offers people free 20-minute bicycle journey with Metrolink lines shut Transport for Greater Manchester have apologised for the disruption across Metrolink Comments News Edward Barnes Local Democracy Reporter 13:30, 20 Jul 2026 U People are being offered free public transport amid Metrolink disruption, with several lines closed across Greater Manchester. The work will end on August 2 Since July 13, passengers who would normally use the Altrincham , Eccles or Trafford Centre Metrolink lines have been forced to use tram replacement buses, or other forms of transport, to get to where they need to be It comes as vital engineering work is carried out as part of a £150m investment to improve the network and upgrade track more than 30 years old . This is to help make a major part of Manchester’s transport network more reliable in the future as trams will be able to operate safel During this period, no trams will run on the Altrincham, Trafford Centre and Eccles lines, including to and from MediaCity, while the East Didsbury and Manchester Airport lines will terminate at Firswood with replacement buses calling at affected stops However, the closure of the lines over the summer has caused significant problems for people commuting and getting across the city. One passenger said it took them an hour and 40 minutes to get from Oldham to Eccles

input.evidence_text: Fires Tintwistle Moor fire update as four helicopters and dozens of firefighters working on scene The fire service said crews are dealing with 'flare-ups' on the moorland near Glossop Comments News Ashlie Blakey Live and breaking news reporter 13:43, The fire service has issued an update on the major blaze at Tintwistle Moor as 65 firefighters work on the scene The fire on the moorland, near Glossop above Woodhead Road, has now been burning for almost four weeks. The Derbyshire Fire and Rescue Service (DFRS) declared a major incident last Monday as parts of the moorland remained engulfed in flames Huge plumes of smoke from the fire continue to billow over Greater Manchester and in surrounding areas. In an update issued today (July 20), the fire service said crews are now dealing with 'flare-ups' on the moorland from overnight 65 firefighters are working at the scene alongside 5 personnel from Derby Mountain Rescue Team and 10 from United Utilities. Four helicopters are being used to carry out water drops on the moorland The A628 Woodhead Pass remains shut to allow for emergency responders to get to and from the scene safely. A car crash on the road last Thursday also sparked a fire on nearby moorland after the vehicle burst into flames

Varley was convicted of murder, GBH, child cruelty and sexual abuse, and handed a whole life order, while McGowan-Fazakerley was jailed for 25 years for a Under UK law if an inquest is suspended and later resumed after a homicide conviction, such as murder, the conclusion of an inquest cannot be inconsistent with the criminal jury’s determination

title: Keir Starmer didn't just lose power, he lost the country - it should be a warning for Andy Burnham

input.lead: Andy Burnham Keir Starmer didn't just lose power, he lost the country - it should be a warning for Andy Burnham A landslide victory was supposed to give Keir Starmer years to reshape Britain.
input.summary: Andy Burnham Keir Starmer didn't just lose power, he lost the country - it should be a warning for Andy Burnham A landslide victory was supposed to give Keir Starmer years to reshape Britain. Instead, in little more than two years, he lost the confide If the smile didn't quite co
input.evidence_text: Andy Burnham Keir Starmer didn't just lose power, he lost the country - it should be a warning for Andy Burnham A landslide victory was supposed to give Keir Starmer years to reshape Britain. Instead, in little more than two years, he lost the confide If the smile didn't quite convince, it's because the manner and speed of his departure were not entirely of his own choosing. There was dignity in the choreography of the farewell, but very little disguising the political reality behind it The departing premier, joined by his wife Lady Victoria Starmer, insisted the country was "stronger and fairer" than when Labour took office. Judging by the mood within his own party, and among an increasingly sceptical electorate, that message hasn’t landed He didn't look entirely convinced of it himself For all the ceremony, there was a sense that this was less a triumphant valedictory and more an acknowledgement that events had overtaken him. Prime Ministers rarely get to decide the terms on which they leave office, and Starmer is no exception. His resignation may have been del The May local elections were the final blow, but the rot had set in long before then

title: Breast cancer patient's race against time to access life-extending drug that's unavailable on NHS

input.lead: Monday 13 July 2026 at 3:43pm Play Brightcove video Sarah and husband Martin has been speaking to ITV Granada Reports’ Emma Sweeney A breast cancer patient from Greater Manchester says she’s devastated and angry that a drug that she believes could extend her life is still not ava
input.summary: Monday 13 July 2026 at 3:43pm Play Brightcove video Sarah and husband Martin has been speaking to ITV Granada Reports’ Emma Sweeney A breast cancer patient from Greater Manchester says she’s devastated and angry that a drug that she believes could extend her life is still not ava
input.evidence_text: Monday 13 July 2026 at 3:43pm Play Brightcove video Sarah and husband Martin has been speaking to ITV Granada Reports’ Emma Sweeney A breast cancer patient from Greater Manchester says she’s devastated and angry that a drug that she believes could extend her life is still not available on the NHS in England Sarah Barchetti, who has been living with secondary breast cancer for seven years, has been treated at The Christie hospital in Manchester throughout her illness. But now, as NHS treatment options dwindle, Sarah has turned to fundraising for private care Sarah said: “It blew our world apart,” recalling her diagnosis in 2019 She added: “I’ve lived with it now for seven years. I’m eternally grateful to The Christie, but we’ve come to a point now where they can’t offer me anything else under the NHS. My team are as frustrated as I am.” Sarah believes the drug Enhertu could help her, but while it is available to NHS patients with HER2-low metastatic breast cancer in Scotland, it is not yet offered in England

input.evidence_text: Friday 10 July 2026 at 6:00pm Anthony McGrath accused Andy Burnham of protecting peodophiles. A bomb hoaxer claimed he would target a town hall after accusing Andy Burnham of “protecting paedophiles" and threatening MPs, a court has heard Anthony McGrath, 57, claimed the bombs would arrive from Russia and added: “Stop protecting #paedophiles @AndyBurnhamGM because the town hall will also be removed.” Posting on X in November 2025, McGrath also threatened Greater Manchester Police headquarters unless they returned property belonging to him, Liverpool Crown Court heard on Friday 10 July In January and February 2024 he sent a series of threatening emails to professionals, including Salford MP Rebecca Long-Bailey and Sir Robert Buckland, who at the time was MP for South Swindon, as well as a judge, solicitor, chartered surveyor and Nat West bank strategist McGrath, of Sale, Greater Manchester, was given a community order after the court heard he was suffering with his mental health at the time and had been sectioned following his arrest Martyn Walsh, prosecuting, said in one email sent to Ms Long-Bailey, McGrath said: “You all messed with the wrong person. My army is much bigger considering I have Russian and Chinese support. We will be armed and shoot to kill.”

input.evidence_text: Friday 10 July 2026 at 8:00am Steven Dossett was treated for stab wounds at the scene and rushed to hospital but died days later. Credit: Google Maps A murder investigation has been launched after a 40-year-old man died in hospital weeks after being stabbed in a "shocking" attack Emergency services were called to Hale Lane in Failsworth at around 9:50pm on Friday, 19 June, following reports of an altercation Steven Dossett was treated for stab wounds at the scene and rushed to hospital. Greater Manchester Police have confirmed that he died from his injuries on Saturday, 4 July Detectives say they are continuing to pursue multiple lines of enquiry Five people - four men and one woman aged between 17 and 45 - were initially arrested on suspicion of grievous bodily harm and assault offences. They have since been released on bail while investigations continue Detective Chief Inspector Anna Barker, the Senior Investigating Officer, said: "This was a shocking attack, which has now evolved into a murder investigation following the death of Steven last week."

title: Andy Burnham wins Makerfield by-election: What happens next with Greater Manchester mayoralty?

input.lead: Friday 19 June 2026 at 1:43pm Andy Burnham has been mayor of Greater Manchester for almost a decade.
input.summary: Friday 19 June 2026 at 1:43pm Andy Burnham has been mayor of Greater Manchester for almost a decade. Credit: PA Images Andy Burnham’s historic victory in the Makerfield by-election sets off a rapid chain reaction for both Greater Manchester and the wider UK political landscape A
input.evidence_text: Friday 19 June 2026 at 1:43pm Andy Burnham has been mayor of Greater Manchester for almost a decade. Credit: PA Images Andy Burnham’s historic victory in the Makerfield by-election sets off a rapid chain reaction for both Greater Manchester and the wider UK political landscape A metro mayor cannot simultaneously serve as an MP, a clear legal and electoral process is now underway to find someone new to take on the role Andy Burnham has won a seat as an MP - but can he still be the Mayor of Greater Manchester? No. Andy Burnham cannot be mayor and a member of parliament. He had seven days - from the moment of his election - to resign from the post which he has held for almost a decade He handed in his resignation on 19 June - triggering the process of the by-election Burnham cannot do both jobs because the Mayor of Greater Manchester holds Police and Crime Commissioner (PCC) powers and UK law strictly dictates that the office holder cannot simultaneously serve as an MP

title: Boost for plans for 500+ homes in Stockport

input.lead: Russell LDP has increased the number of homes it is proposing to build in Stockport.
input.summary: Russell LDP has increased the number of homes it is proposing to build in Stockport. Credit: via planning documents Developer Russell LDP has increased the number of homes it plans to build on 75 acres off Chester Road in Woodford, now that the site has been allocated for housing
input.evidence_text: Russell LDP has increased the number of homes it is proposing to build in Stockport. Credit: via planning documents Developer Russell LDP has increased the number of homes it plans to build on 75 acres off Chester Road in Woodford, now that the site has been allocated for housing in the council’s draft local plan Russell LDP and landowner JAG Woodford submitted plans for 554 homes to Stockport Council last year. At the time, the site was not allocated for development and the council had previously declared it was unwilling to release any Green Belt for housing However, in the intervening months, the local authority has earmarked the site for around 545 homes in the latest draft of its emerging local plan Subsequently, Russell LDP has tweaked its plans to provide 567 homes in total, including 423 standard homes and 144 care units – an increase of 13 homes compared to the original plan Stantec is advising on planning matters. To learn more about the development, search for reference number DC/096927 on Stockport Council’s planning portal

input.lead: Hope Architects designed the outline project for Peel Land at Boothstown.
input.summary: Hope Architects designed the outline project for Peel Land at Boothstown. Credit: via Lexington Salford City Council has approved plans for the development of 53 acres south of Leigh Road next to the horticultural destination Peel Land submitted plans for the site three years ago
input.evidence_text: Hope Architects designed the outline project for Peel Land at Boothstown. Credit: via Lexington Salford City Council has approved plans for the development of 53 acres south of Leigh Road next to the horticultural destination Peel Land submitted plans for the site three years ago and has now secured outline approval for 282 homes, in line with the land’s release from the Green Belt in the Places for Everyone joint spatial plan for Greater Manchester The plans approved by Salford today [Thursday] were lodged following a revision of an earlier proposal for 350 homes. At the time, Peel proposed 20% on-site affordable homes and the equivalent of a further 30% secured through a financial contribution However, this has since been revised to 10% on site and 40% through financial contribution. As a result, 28 affordable homes will be delivered in Boothstown with a potential for 113 to be built elsewhere across the city A spokesperson for Peel Land described the scheme as a “truly one-of-a-kind development”

title: Refugee's amazing journey from war zone to graduatio - About Manchester

input.lead: A woman who fled Liberia’s brutal civil war as a young adult, lost both parents and spent years living in refugee camps is to graduate from The University of Manchester this month with a 2:1 BSc in public health(20/07/26).
input.summary: A woman who fled Liberia’s brutal civil war as a young adult, lost both parents and spent years living in refugee camps is to graduate from The University of Manchester this month with a 2:1 BSc in public health(20/07/26). In December 1990 Makagbeh Princess Sesay crossed into nei
input.evidence_text: A woman who fled Liberia’s brutal civil war as a young adult, lost both parents and spent years living in refugee camps is to graduate from The University of Manchester this month with a 2:1 BSc in public health(20/07/26) In December 1990 Makagbeh Princess Sesay crossed into neighbouring Guinea (Guinea-Conakry ) in a truck with her family after fighting forced them to flee their home during the Liberian civil war Her father died in Liberia, not long after the civil war began. After that, she also lost her mother in a small Guinean border town called Koyama, located near the Liberia–Guinea border Left to navigate life as a refugee, she grew up in communities hosting thousands of displaced Liberians in the towns of Macenta, Guéckédou and Kissidougou She witnessed first-hand the hardships faced by refugee families, including poverty, food shortages, poor sanitation, limited access to healthcare and disrupted education In Guéckédou, she witnessed vicious attacks by Liberian rebel forces in 2000 and many people died across the whole town in appalling circumstances

title: Money Matters: Jason Moss on why it's time for your financial spring clean

input.lead: Jason is an Independent Financial Adviser at Harvest Associates
input.summary: Jason is an Independent Financial Adviser at Harvest Associates
input.evidence_text: Jason is an Independent Financial Adviser at Harvest Associates Share on WhatsApp Share via Email Jason Moss is an Independent Financial Adviser at Harvest Associates and writes a regular column for Altrincham Today on pensions, investment planning and making the most of your money Reflecting on 2025, our family grew from three to four with the arrival of our second daughter. Moments like that put everything into perspective - they remind you what's truly important. They also bring back sleep deprivation, but hey, more chaos equals more love We only have one life, and we don't know how long we're here for. That's why I always try to remind my clients: make the most of it. Enjoy it. Live your best life For lots of people, the idea of retirement is genuinely frightening. Two things tend to hold people back: the money question and the identity question Moving from a regular income to living off savings and pensions feels uncertain. Will it be enough? title: Surgeons, architects and beauticians: the Trafalgar House mix

input.lead: It attracts a tenant mix that reflects just how broad the building's appeal has become
input.summary: It attracts a tenant mix that reflects just how broad the building's appeal has become
input.evidence_text: It attracts a tenant mix that reflects just how broad the building's appeal has become Share on WhatsApp Share via Email A medico-legal specialist, an architect-engineer partnership and an experienced beautician – not an obvious combination, but they've all found a place to call home at Trafalgar House in Altrincham The building on Manchester Road has become one of Altrincham's most talked-about business addresses Transformed at a cost of £3 million by local family business Selbourne Group, it offers high-spec office and studio space at prices well below the city centre – and has attracted a tenant mix that reflects just how broad the appeal has become Of the three businesses we visited, only one has been at Trafalgar House long enough to renew a lease. Laurie Bellis, who runs The Treatment Room from Studio 3, is now into her third year – and shows no sign of going anywhere Laurie has been in the beauty and wellness industry for three decades, working in some of the best spas and clinics in the country, including Naked Health in London, which she describes as one of the first of its kind in the UK

title: Leaky dams on Holcombe Moor to stop flooding and wildfires

input.summary: The wooden dams have been installed in gullies by The National Trust and United Utilities across Holcombe Moor, part of the Stubbins Estate above Ramsbottom Nik Taylor, 57, a National Trust Ranger for Holcombe Moor and the Stubbins Estate, said: "Keeping the moors wet by raising
input.evidence_text: The wooden dams have been installed in gullies by The National Trust and United Utilities across Holcombe Moor, part of the Stubbins Estate above Ramsbottom Nik Taylor, 57, a National Trust Ranger for Holcombe Moor and the Stubbins Estate, said: "Keeping the moors wet by raising ground water levels is really important when we're in a period of hot weather because it can help create fire-breaks." United Utilities said using the peat land as "a natural sponge" can keep water on the moors giving multiple benefits Taylor said: "We use leaky dams to control the flow of water into the River Irwell and it's an essential part of the restoration of our peat lands More than 1,000 acres of land at Holcombe Moor was transferred from the Ministry of Defence to the National Trust in 1994 The dams are designed to hold back the flow of rainwater which lands on the moors and subsequently enters the River Irwell catchment area

When landscapes experience several consecutive days of dry weather and little or no rain, plants and soils lose moisture faster than they can recover. Most wildfires start due to human activities and are a risk to communities, emergency services as well as increasing pre All smoke is potentially harmful to your health. title: Smoke from wildfire affecting air quality in some areas

input.lead: Greater Manchester Fire and Rescue Service (GMFRS) and partner agencies continue to respond to the moorland fire near Dovestone Reservoir
input.summary: Greater Manchester Fire and Rescue Service (GMFRS) and partner agencies continue to respond to the moorland fire near Dovestone Reservoir
input.evidence_text: Greater Manchester Fire and Rescue Service (GMFRS) and partner agencies continue to respond to the moorland fire near Dovestone Reservoir Strong winds have carried smoke across parts of Greater Manchester, including Manchester Residents should avoid areas affected by smoke wherever possible. If smoke is visible in your area, stay indoors with doors and windows closed. This is the most effective way to reduce exposure to wildfire smoke Wildfire smoke can irritate the eyes, nose, throat and lungs. Most healthy people will only experience temporary symptoms, but smoke can have a greater impact on babies and young children, older people, pregnant people, and those with existing heart or lung conditions For most people any symptoms will be temporary and will quickly disappear once you are away from the smoke. However, if symptoms persist or get worse, you should seek health advice Symptoms may include irritated eyes and airways, coughing, wheezing, shortness of breath and chest discomfort. title: Oldham bucks national trend as more young people choose education and training

input.evidence_text: Oldham has recorded participation rates above both regional and national averages while significantly reducing the number of young people who are not in education, employment or training (NEET) The latest Department for Education (DfE) Local Authority Participation and NEET figures for the 2025/26 academic year show that 93.2 per cent of Oldham's 16 and 17-year-olds are participating in education and training, an increase of 2.8 percentage points on the previous year. T At the same time, Oldham's combined NEET and Not Known (NK) rate has fallen to 4.6%, down 1.1 percentage points from 2024/25. This is significantly lower than both the North West average of 5.5% and the England average of 5.8% The figures also highlight Oldham's strong performance, in partnership with the charity Positive Steps, in tracking and supporting young people Positive Steps provides young people with support, careers advice and guidance after school, or between 19 and 25 if they have an Education Healthcare Plan (EHCP), helping them to move into education, training or employment Only 59 young people out of 7,177 are currently unaccounted for – 0.8 per cent of the borough's young people. This is well below the North West average of 1.3 per cent and the England average of 2.4 per cent

title: Encouraging decline in youth smoking and vaping across Wigan Borough

input.evidence_text: Wigan Borough is seeing a positive decline in the number of young people smoking and regularly vaping, according to a recent Trading Standards survey Following national concerns about youth smoking and vaping, Wigan Council has made it a priority to reduce these figures locally and protect the health of our children and young people With more than 1,000 school children aged 14-17 included in the Trading Standards survey, the findings show the number of young people who have tried vaping dropped from around 44 per cent in 2023 to 18 per cent in 2026 The survey, carried out between November 2025 and February 2026, also highlights that smoking is at an all-time low, with around 97 per cent reporting to have never tried smoking Councillor Keith Cunliffe, Deputy Leader of Wigan Council and cabinet portfolio holder for adult social care and health, said: “I’m really pleased to see smoking and vaping rates among young people in Wigan Borough continuing to fall “Reducing tobacco and nicotine use among young people is one of the most effective ways to prevent long-term ill health and reduce health inequalities in our communities

input.evidence_text: The Girls (PG) A young girl finds herself torn between duty and desire in this stunning Sri Lankan coming-of-age drama. Sat 18 Jul - Wed 22 Jul Romance Drama Cinema Book Now Sat 18 Jul - Wed 22 Jul Book Now Tue 21 Jul 13:20 Tickets Wed 22 Jul 20:20 Tickets Sat 18 Jul 13:15 Past e Kusum, a poor but studious village girl who cleans the house of a wealthy family, sparks a connection with Nimal, the family’s prized son. Soma, Kusum’s younger sister, pursues beauty pageants and dreams of acting, believing it’s her best chance of a better life. What unfolds is Sumitra Peries, known as ‘the poetess of Sri Lankan cinema’, became the country’s first female director with this astonishing debut. She draws out natural, affecting performances from her cast, particularly Vasanthi Chathurani, who was still at school when she played Kusum – the The Girls was crowned the Outstanding Film of the Year at the 1978 London Film Festival. Beautifully restored in digital 4K, it feels just as fresh, illuminating and moving today (Stills courtesy of Film Heritage Foundation) Céline Sciamma’s sublime modern fairytale about mother-daughter relationships

title: Marina Abramović: Balkan Erotic Epic | Aviva Studios

input.lead: Experience the raw, unfiltered power of Balkan folklore in a new immersive show from the world’s most famous performance artist Balkan Erotic Epic is a bold new performance work created by the iconic artist Marina Abramović .
input.summary: Experience the raw, unfiltered power of Balkan folklore in a new immersive show from the world’s most famous performance artist Balkan Erotic Epic is a bold new performance work created by the iconic artist Marina Abramović . Regarded as the world’s most influential performance a
input.evidence_text: Experience the raw, unfiltered power of Balkan folklore in a new immersive show from the world’s most famous performance artist Balkan Erotic Epic is a bold new performance work created by the iconic artist Marina Abramović . Regarded as the world’s most influential performance artist, Abramović’s transformative work has reached millions of people – from opening the Pyramid Stage at Glastonbury Festival t A four-hour ritual where ancient myth meets performance art, Balkan Erotic Epic explores the eroticism, spirituality and traditions of Abramović’s homeland through 13 visceral scenes Brought to life by a cast of over 70 performers, dancers, musicians and singers, the experience unfolds across Aviva Studios. Audiences are free to choose their own path – with pop-up encounters punctuating the action, bringing bodies together in dance, song and ritual Watch Fertility Rite , a fevered ritual where bodies writhe against the ground in a desperate call for fertility. Witness Massaging the Breast where women gesticulate over graves to awaken the earth. In Scaring the Gods , women bare themselves to the sky to banish storms. Listen Unafraid to push the boundaries of eroticism and performance art, Balkan Erotic Epic asks its audience a timeless question: What are our bodies truly for?

We’re inviting young people to explore music for film and compose their own soundtrack this summer! Whether your child already attends our Saturday Music Club , or just wants to try something new, our Young Musician’s Summer School is suitable for all abilities! . . . . . Across this 5 day course, we’ll be exploring music for film, and working on composing music for the silent film ‘A Trip to the Moon’. The course will conclude with a live performance soundtracking the film for family and friends. . . . . We welcome all abilities and instruments to take part in this course. You can bring your own instruments, but we have keyboards, guitars, bass, violins, drums and percussion on site which can be used if you don’t have an instrument.. . . Snacks and drinks provided, and pizzas on the final day. Please bring your own packed lunch to sessions Monday-Thursday.. . Dates: Monday 17th – Friday 21st August. Times: 10:00-16:30 Monday-Thursday, then a performance for friends and family on Friday at 13:00 (students will be expected to come in at 10:00 sharp on the Friday).. Ages: 12-18 years old. Cost: £150 for the full course. Bursaries are available for those on lower income – please email learning@bandonthewall.org to arrange this.. . Spaces are limited – Book here . . . title: Mike Rice

input.lead: RNCM | Live Nation in association with UTA | 2026-09-27 | tickets
input.summary: RNCM | Live Nation in association with UTA | 2026-09-27 | tickets
input.evidence_text: There’s a cruelty to Mike Rice, a wickedness. In this show Mike talks about doing psychedelics with his brother, an entanglement with a Spanish Sausage Dog named Nino, and many other such rotten adventures ‘ Ireland has yet another legendary comedian ‘ – Dan Nightingale Irish farmer’s son, and general Nasty Character Mike Rice is one of Ireland and the UK’s fastest rising comedy stars. Having started comedy in 2016 Mike has amassed a large online following from podcast and stand-up clips, regularly getting millions of views on various platforms Mike began stand-up comedy in Chicago, where within two years he was working regularly at the most prestigious clubs in the city, including The Laugh Factory. He has since returned home because his mother made him, and has been quickly rising in the Irish and European scene, play Mike brought his first solo show The Best Boy in Ireland to the Edinburgh Fringe Festival 2018, in what was a sold-out run, and performed that same show to sold-out rooms at the Perth Fringe Festival in 2019 As well as co-hosting Mike & Vittorio’s Guide to Parenting with Vittorio Angelone, and Big Mike and The Chief with Rob Moriarty, Mike has appeared multiple times on smash hit podcast Have a Word and released his first comedy special in October 2023

title: Спектакль "Скамейка"

input.lead: Друзья, а вы давно ощущали настоящую магию театра?
input.summary: Друзья, а вы давно ощущали настоящую магию театра? 29 ноября 2026 года в Logan Hall — легендарная «Скамейка» с Алексеем Паниным
input.evidence_text: Спектакль «Скамейка» в Лондоне | Алексей Панин | 29 ноября 2026 :: EventFirst Home double_arrow Events double_arrow Спектакль "Скамейка" On sale Спектакль "Скамейка" schedule 29 Nov 2026 Sunday, 19:00 location_on Logan Hall 20 Bedford Way, London WC1H 0AL balcony 6:00 pm gpp_maybe NO REFUNDS Buy On MAP Buy Tickets Logan Hall £30.00 Select Logan Hall £35.00 Select Logan Hall £40.00 Select Logan Hall £45.00 Select Logan Hall £50.00 Select Logan Hall £55.00 Select Logan Hall £60.00 Select Logan Hall £65.00 Sold Out Logan Hall £70.00 Select Logan Hall £80.00 Select Logan Hall £85.00 Sold Out Logan Hall £90.00 Select Друзья, а вы давно ощущали настоящую магию театра? 29 ноября 2026 года в Logan Hall — легендарная «Скамейка» с Алексеем Паниным. После аншлагов в Европе и США спектакль впервые приезжает в Лондон. 📍 University College London, Level 1, 20 Bedford Way, London WC1H 0AL 🕕 DOORS: 18:00 🕖 SHOW: 19:00 «Скамейка» — знаменитая пьеса, когда-то блистательно сыгранная Олегом Ефремовым и Татьяной Дорониной на сцене МХАТа, а затем покорившая театральные площадки по всему миру. Это история о мужчине и женщине. О случайной встрече, которая постепенно превращается в откровенный разговор — честный, смешной, ранимый и очень человеческий. О том моменте, когда между людьми исчезают роли и, наконец, становится невозможно притворяться. На сцене — Алексей Панин и Ольга Силина. Тонкая актерская игра, живая энергия и удивительно узнаваемая история, в которой каждый услышит что-то своё. “Скамейка” — спектакль, после которого хочется немного помолчать и еще долго думать о главном. Если вам не хватает настоящего театра — этот вечер нельзя пропустить. Количество билетов ограничено. Продолжительность спектакля — 2 часа с антрактом. settings Loading venue plan... shopping_cart Your tickets: 0

To celebrate, the pizzeria is giving away 3,000 pizzas on the house a The new pizzeria, located at 237-239 Monton Road , will transform the former Bennetts Furnishers store that has been vacant since 2022, with work now officially underway. The spacious venue will mark Rudy’s 41st restaurant to date Since launching in Manchester’s Ancoats in 2015, Rudy’s has grown into one of the UK’s most loved pizzerias, celebrated for its light, double fermented dough and unwavering dedication to true Neopolitan craft Testament to its authenticity, Rudy’s is the largest organisation in the world to be accredited by the Associazione Verace Pizza Napoletana (AVPN), the international body that protects and promotes traditional Neapolitan pizza making Rudy’s has also been recognised among the World’s 50 Best Pizzeria, ranking 23rd globally for 2025. Each pizza, from the classic Margherita to the fiery Calabrese, is made to AVPN standards using traditional techniques and ingredients sourced directly from Italy To claim one of 3,000 complimentary pizzas, Monton locals can sign up via this link , where they will be able to choose from Rudy’s favourites such as its Cinghiale , Margherita Con Bufala, and Triple Pepperoni to enjoy at the new opening this summer

title: Governing AI: Building trust, managing risk, and staying compliant | Manchester Digital

input.lead: Wednesday, 22nd July at 15:00 BST | 16:00 CET / SAST AI adoption is accelerating across every industry, but innovation without governance introduces new risks.
input.summary: Wednesday, 22nd July at 15:00 BST | 16:00 CET / SAST AI adoption is accelerating across every industry, but innovation without governance introduces new risks. From sensitive data being shared with public AI tools to evolving regulatory requirements, organisations must ensure AI
input.evidence_text: Governing AI: Building trust, managing risk, and staying compliant | Manchester Digital Governing AI: Building trust, managing risk, and staying compliant View Profile View More Events 22 July 2026 15:00 - 15:45 Book Now Wednesday, 22nd July at 15:00 BST | 16:00 CET / SAST 08:00 MTD | 10:00 EST | 11:00 AST Speakers Brian Martin , Director of Product Management, Integrity360 Seán Brennan , CRA Practice Manager, Integrity360 AI adoption is accelerating across every industry, but innovation without governance introduces new risks. From sensitive data being shared with public AI tools to evolving regulatory requirements, organisations must ensure AI is being deployed responsibly, securely and with appropriate oversight. In this webinar, Brian Martin is joined by Seán Brennan, CRA Practice Manager, Integrity360, to explore how organisations can confidently adopt AI while managing governance, risk and compliance. Drawing on emerging frameworks including ISO/IEC 42001, the NIST AI Risk Management Framework, and the EU AI Act, this session will provide practical guidance on establishing governance for both internally developed AI solutions and the use of public AI platforms. Attendees will gain insight into managing AI-related risks, protecting sensitive information, meeting emerging regulatory expectations, and creating a framework that enables innovation without compromising security or compliance. Join us for a practical discussion on how to build effective AI governance that balances innovation with trust, accountability and risk management. What you will learn: Why AI governance has become a business and regulatory priority The key risks organisations face as AI adoption accelerates How ISO/IEC 42001 provides a framework for governing AI responsibly The role of the NIST AI

title: Day one of PM Burnham - low on detail, high on hope. So what are regional industry's hopes?

input.lead: The former Greater Manchester Mayor, and first PM to hail from the North in over 50 years since Huddersfield native Harold Wilson took up the hotseat for a second time in 1974, is finally PM.
input.summary: The former Greater Manchester Mayor, and first PM to hail from the North in over 50 years since Huddersfield native Harold Wilson took up the hotseat for a second time in 1974, is finally PM. What do Northern agencies and creatives make of it all?
input.evidence_text: Day one of PM Burnham – low on detail, high on hope. So what are regional industry's hopes? - Prolific North Skip to content Agency Laura Ashley and Barbour’s retail and showroom designer makes PR agency appointment Brand Tributes paid to Liverpool, Newcastle and Man City legend Kevin Keegan TV, Film & Streaming BBC dominates as World Cup Final peaks at 17m viewers across BBC and ITV Day one of PM Burnham – low on detail, high on hope. So what are regional industry’s hopes? Agency , Creative by Chris Newbould July 20, 2026 It’s day one of the long-anticipated Andy Burnham premiership , and his debut speech was perhaps more memorable for his decision not to use a lecturn outside Number 10 Downing Street to address the nation, in contrast with his many recent predecessors, a clear nod to his desire to “do things differently” and be a man of the people and natural communicator rather than a faceless member of the much-maligned “establishment,” although he did uncharacteristically wear a suit and tie, as pictured (in fairness though, he had just been at Buckingham Palace with the King.) Indeed, in the absence of solid news lines, the lecturn issue was granted almost as many broadcast minutes on BBC News as his, admittedly scant, policy announcements later in the day, and many of us will now full much better educated on the style, purpose and development of the lecturn, and the lecturn choices of past PMs as a result. In short (which his opener certainly was), Burnham – the former Greater Manchester Mayor and first PM to hail from the North in over 50 years since Huddersfield native Harold Wilson took up the hotseat for a second time in 1974 – largely gave an edited highlights of what he had already said when he was appointed Labour leader on Friday: More council houses, imp

title: Debenhams Group faces £10m new legal claims as more investors join £245m High Court action

input.lead: Another 11 institutional investors to the Manchester-based Debenhams Group, formerly known as boohoo Group, have joined a High Court lawsuit, with the latest claims bringing the value of the compensation sought to £245m, according to The Telegraph
input.summary: Another 11 institutional investors to the Manchester-based Debenhams Group, formerly known as boohoo Group, have joined a High Court lawsuit, with the latest claims bringing the value of the compensation sought to £245m, according to The Telegraph
input.evidence_text: Debenhams Group faces £10m new legal claims as more investors join £245m High Court action - Prolific North Skip to content Agency Laura Ashley and Barbour’s retail and showroom designer makes PR agency appointment Brand Tributes paid to Liverpool, Newcastle and Man City legend Kevin Keegan TV, Film & Streaming BBC dominates as World Cup Final peaks at 17m viewers across BBC and ITV Debenhams Group faces £10m new legal claims as more investors join £245m High Court action Ecommerce by Chris Newbould July 20, 2026 Another 11 institutional investors to the Manchester-based Debenhams Group , formerly known as boohoo Group , have joined a High Court lawsuit, with the latest claims bringing the value of the compensation sought to £245m, according to The Telegraph. This follows an exposé in July 2020, which saw an undercover investigation into Boohoo’s UK supply chain unveil the use of sweatshops, as well as workers being paid below the UK minimum wage in unsafe working conditions. In particular, the BBC Panorama investigation , which followed a reporter spending 10 weeks in an undercover role at Boohoo’s Manchester headquarters, claimed that executives at the etailer and fast fashion giant knew it was using Leicester-based sweatshops to make its clothes. The new investors who have brought claims against the group include the Bank of Korea and Netherlands-based private sector corporate pension fund for Shell employees Stichting Shell Pensioenfonds (SSPF). Simon joined the business in September 2025 as head of portfolio and will now oversee all of Northern Gritstone's investment activities. An experienced venture capital and private equity investor, he previously led the Sustainable Growth Funds in the North at Bridges. Simon said: “I’m delighted to be appointed as Northern Gritstone’s new chief investment officer. “In my previous role as head of portfolio I have seen first-hand the power of Northern Gritstone’s investment model not just to create the world leading businesses of tomorrow but the fundamental impact that this success can have on the local, regional and national economies in which we operate.” The company has also expanded its investment team with the appointment of James Bellis as portfolio director and Narjeet Singh as investment manager. James joins from global advisory firm Teneo, while Narjeet has moved from Lincoln International’s technology M&A team. Since launching in 2022, Northern Gritstone has reviewed more than 1500 investment opportunities and backed 52 life sciences and technology businesses across the North of England. The latest appointments reflect the company’s continued investment in its leadership and investment capabilities as it grows its portfolio of university spinouts and high-growth businesses. input.summary: Families across Greater Manchester are invited to celebrate the opening of Manchester’s newest city-centre urban park as Into the Wild, a free family nature festival, comes to St Catherine’s Wood on Fri 24 Jul Running from 11am – 6pm, the event marks the public opening of the new
input.evidence_text: Families across Greater Manchester are invited to celebrate the opening of Manchester’s newest city-centre urban park as Into the Wild, a free family nature festival, comes to St Catherine’s Wood on Fri 24 Jul Running from 11am – 6pm, the event marks the public opening of the newly restored woodland. Designed to help families reconnect with nature, Into the Wild is packed with free activities for all ages Follow the Archie and Irky Nature Trail, enjoy live steel pan music from Zola Steel Pan, get hands-on with nature-inspired crafts, create wildflower seed bombs, help build a giant bug hotel and join a woodland BioBlitz with Plant MCR to uncover the wildlife that calls St Catherin While all activities are free, advance booking is recommended for the BioBlitz workshop and the Moodswings mindfulness walk due to limited capacity. Free tickets can be booked online here The event marks the first opportunity for the public to experience St Catherine’s Wood, which has been transformed as part of the Victoria North regeneration programme into a welcoming urban park that aims to reconnect local communities with nature Be the first to hear about all the best events and news in Manchester

title: REVIEW: Royal Exchange's Fun Home offers a tender, exquisitely observed exploration of identity and belonging

input.evidence_text: Running throughout the month under the direction of Sarah Frankcom, Fun Home takes over the Royal Exchange with the measured assurance of a modern classic Adapted from Alison Bechdel’s celebrated graphic memoir, the five-time Tony Award-winning musical charts the cartoonist’s childhood, adolescence and adulthood simultaneously, weaving memories of family life, first love and long-buried grief into an intimate, funny and profoundly Fun Home unfolds as a collage of recollections, with three performers sharing the role of Alison as she pieces together the story of an emotionally elusive father who lives a clandestine life as a gay man. Where many other plays of as many moving parts swiftly become a convoluted Jodie McNee brings layered composure and warmth to the adult Alison – an omnipotent narrator guiding the audience through memories of her sexual awakening that are by turns joyful, awkward and devastating – while Alice Audrey O’Hanlon is irresistibly charming as the exuberant stu Opposite them, decorated actor Nigel Harman gives Bruce Bechdel extraordinary depth, balancing charisma, fatherly duty and the simmering volatility of long-held torment without reducing the character to cliched villainy or victimhood. His portrait of a man crushed beneath secrecy Despite its setting in a funeral home and the shadow of inevitable tragedy, Fun Home sustains a life-affirming message of optimism. The exuberant Come to the Fun Home brims with irreverent humour, while Tesori’s score repeatedly punctures melancholy with compassion, wit and sever

title: An acclaimed rock opera set in the aftermath of the Peterloo Massacre comes to Manchester this month

input.lead: An acclaimed rock opera set in the aftermath of the Peterloo Massacre comes to Manchester later this month Fusing progressive folk rock, classic rock, indie and blues, The Reformers’ Peterloo in Rock takes place immediately after the events of 1819, guiding the audience through p
input.summary: An acclaimed rock opera set in the aftermath of the Peterloo Massacre comes to Manchester later this month Fusing progressive folk rock, classic rock, indie and blues, The Reformers’ Peterloo in Rock takes place immediately after the events of 1819, guiding the audience through p
input.evidence_text: An acclaimed rock opera set in the aftermath of the Peterloo Massacre comes to Manchester later this month Fusing progressive folk rock, classic rock, indie and blues, The Reformers’ Peterloo in Rock takes place immediately after the events of 1819, guiding the audience through pivotal reforms of the nineteenth and twentieth centuries and culminating in the present day The principal writer and composer, Robert (Bob) Carter, explains that the early reformers were looking for many things that have now been changed, such as fairer distribution of parliamentary seats, a voice for the working people of the north, universal suffrage, including a vote Carter also goes on to poignantly highlight that this struggle for equality persists as an issue today, a theme that resonates through the music The show features a striking complementary visual presentation created by Manchester graphic novelist Polyp ( Peterloo , Witnesses to a Massacre; Courage ), who has contributed to a fully immersive audio-visual experience Following a run of sold-out shows last autumn, Peterloo in Rock comes to the Mechanics Institute in Manchester on Sat 25 Jul. Tickets and more information can be found below; use the code HISTORIES 2026 to claim an exclusive discount

The square itself ha From here, the tour takes you through the city’s heart, down the colourful streets of the Gay Village and the calming Sackville Gardens. It is here that you will see the monuments that honour Manchester’s role in the fight for equality and the city’s pioneering LGBTQ+ community a This guided tour is filled with monuments and tales from many moons ago. The Medieval Quarter is the perfect place to soak in all that history. It is here where you will see the oldest public library in the English-speaking world, Chetham’s Library . Housed in a beautiful sandsto During all this walking, your guide will point out local street art, hidden corners and stories you’d easily miss on your own.
